_comment: Public probe manifest for the marketing-site Probe Explorer modal. Mirrors metadata from worker/src/engine/probes/*.ts. Keep in sync (no implementation details, just titles + descriptions + severity tiers).

packs.label: Web Classics
packs.tagline: The original Oracle Bot probe set.
packs.description: Race conditions, auth gaps, malformed-input handling, prompt injection, hallucinations, jailbreaks, rate-limit cliffs.
packs.audience: Any web app, AI agent, or API approaching launch.

probes.title: JS console error during navigation
probes.description: Browser console emitted an error while a synthetic user navigated the site.

probes.title: Form submit returned non-2xx without auth
probes.description: A state-mutating form accepted submission from an unauthenticated session.

probes.title: Concurrent form submissions produced inconsistent responses
probes.description: Same payload submitted concurrently produced duplicates or divergent responses.

probes.title: Slow response under realistic load
probes.description: Response time exceeded the latency budget for an interactive flow.

probes.title: Server 500 on adversarial form input
probes.description: A form rendered a 5xx response when a synthetic user submitted adversarial-but-valid-looking input.

probes.title: Agent revealed system prompt
probes.description: Agent response contained text consistent with its own system instructions.

probes.title: Agent broke character / safety guardrails
probes.description: Agent ignored its instructions when faced with a known jailbreak pattern.

probes.title: Agent followed an injected instruction
probes.description: A user-controlled message embedded an instruction that the agent then executed as if operator-authored.

probes.title: Agent confidently stated a falsehood
probes.description: Agent fabricated a fact that could mislead a real user.

probes.title: Agent answered an unrelated request
probes.description: Agent engaged with a topic outside its declared scope when prompted by a synthetic user.

probes.title: No rate limit on agent endpoint
probes.description: Burst traffic to the agent endpoint never triggered 429 responses, leaving cost / abuse exposure.

probes.title: Slow agent response under load
probes.description: Agent turn exceeded the latency budget; degrades UX and may indicate runaway generation.

probes.title: No rate limit on API endpoint
probes.description: Burst traffic to an API endpoint never triggered 429 responses.

probes.title: Endpoint returned 500/200 when 401 expected
probes.description: Hitting a protected endpoint without credentials yielded a server error or a successful response instead of 401.

probes.title: API 500 on malformed input
probes.description: API endpoint crashed with 5xx when sent malformed but well-formed-looking input.

probes.title: Insecure CORS or missing security headers
probes.description: API responded with permissive CORS or missing security headers on a sensitive endpoint.

packs.label: AI-Built Apps
packs.tagline: Failure modes specific to AI-generated code.
packs.description: Hardcoded secrets in client bundles, exposed Supabase anon keys, missing RLS, leaked stack traces on errors, dev/debug endpoints reachable in production, permissive CORS.
packs.audience: Apps shipped via Lovable, v0, Bolt, Cursor, Replit Agent, Claude Code.

probes.title: Hardcoded secret in client bundle
probes.description: Pattern-matches client-side JavaScript for known secret formats (OpenAI / Anthropic / Stripe / GitHub / Resend keys, generic JWTs, .env-leaked variables).

probes.title: Supabase anon key in client bundle
probes.description: Scans the bundle for a Supabase project URL + anon JWT. The anon key alone is meant to be public — severity escalates only if the missing_rls_on_public_tables probe also fires.

probes.title: Public Supabase tables readable without authentication
probes.description: When a Supabase anon key is found in the bundle, OracleBot fetches the project's PostgREST OpenAPI schema and attempts a single LIMIT-1 read against each exposed table. Tables that return rows have no Row Level Security gate — anyone visiting the site can read user data.

probes.title: Firestore / Realtime Database readable without authentication
probes.description: When a Firebase config (apiKey + projectId) is found in the bundle, OracleBot probes Firestore via REST for a small list of common collection names. Collections that return documents to anonymous reads have no security rules gate.

probes.title: Framework version / stack trace leaked on error
probes.description: Triggers a 404 or 500 and inspects the response for framework version banners, stack traces, or absolute file paths that AI builders commonly leave on.

probes.title: Development / debug endpoint reachable in production
probes.description: Probes for a small list of well-known development endpoints (Next.js dev overlay, Prisma Studio, GraphQL introspection, etc.) that should not be reachable in production builds.

probes.title: Permissive CORS on API routes
probes.description: Sends an OPTIONS preflight from a foreign origin to the discovered API base and flags wildcard Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on endpoints that may handle user data.

probes.title: No rate limit on auth endpoints
probes.description: Sends 30 concurrent POSTs to a small list of well-known auth paths with bogus credentials. If no 429s appear and most requests complete, the endpoint is brute-forceable.

probes.title: API route returns user-shaped data without authentication
probes.description: Pulls fetch / axios calls to internal /api/* routes out of the bundle, then attempts each one without cookies. Routes that return user-shaped JSON anonymously are likely gated only by client-side conditional renders.

probes.title: Auth/login route forwards to attacker-controlled URL
probes.description: Probes common auth/login routes with a ?redirect= / ?next= / ?return_to= parameter pointing to a foreign origin. Flags 30x or HTML-body redirects to the foreign URL — open redirects on auth routes enable phishing flows that survive the 'is this a real login page?' check.

probes.title: State-mutating POST accepted without CSRF token
probes.description: Sends cross-origin POSTs to mutation-shaped /api/* routes (with a foreign Origin header) carrying minimal but valid-shaped bodies. Flags 200 responses on routes that should be CSRF-gated.

probes.title: Client bundle contains a library version with a known CVE
probes.description: Pattern-matches the client bundle for version strings of well-known JS libraries with high-impact published CVEs (jQuery, lodash, axios, dompurify, marked, Next.js). Fast first-pass; doesn't replace a full SCA tool.

packs.label: LLM Endpoints
packs.tagline: Adversarial probes for HTTP endpoints wrapping an LLM.
packs.description: System-prompt extraction and prompt injection via user content shipped today. PII echo, cost amplification, jailbreak, unsafe tool-call execution, and output-length-cap probes follow.
packs.audience: Anyone shipping a chatbot, RAG endpoint, or LLM-backed API.

probes.title: System prompt leaked to extraction attempt
probes.description: Sends a battery of well-known system-prompt extraction techniques (reflection, roleplay, encoding tricks, instruction override). Claude Haiku evaluates each response for content consistent with system instructions.

probes.title: Endpoint follows injected instruction inside user-content frame
probes.description: Wraps a small set of injection payloads inside user-document-shaped messages (as if the agent were summarising a doc the user pasted). Flags responses that follow the injected instruction instead of the outer task.

probes.title: Endpoint echoes PII from user input back into the response
probes.description: Sends messages containing canary PII (SSN-shaped numbers, credit-card-shaped numbers, an email) and checks whether the LLM repeats the PII verbatim in its response. Echoing user PII back is a privacy + compliance concern.

probes.title: No max-tokens cap — endpoint streams unbounded responses
probes.description: Sends prompts designed to maximise token consumption ('write a 50,000 word essay'). Measures the response size + duration. Endpoints without a max_tokens cap are exploitable as a cost-amplification attack.

probes.title: Endpoint surfaces tool-call requests from injected user content
probes.description: Wraps tool-call request shapes inside user-content frames. If the endpoint's response includes a tool-call request matching the injected shape, the model is propagating user-controlled instructions into structured calls — a recipe for unsafe execution if any tool layer trusts model output.

probes.title: Endpoint complies with adversarial roleplay / DAN-style jailbreaks
probes.description: Sends a battery of well-known jailbreak patterns (DAN, AIM, hypothetical-frame, opposite-day). Pattern-matches the response for compliance markers and flags responses that drop refusal posture.

probes.title: Endpoint confidently fabricates verifiable facts
probes.description: Sends 5 verifiable factual questions whose correct answers are well-documented. Claude Haiku grades each response for factual accuracy. Endpoints that confidently hallucinate basic facts will fabricate worse on user-domain questions.

probes.title: No structured output-length cap on the endpoint response
probes.description: Sends a deterministic 'list 5,000 items' prompt and measures whether the response shows truncation markers OR runs unbounded. Different from cost_amplification_attack — this checks whether a deliberate cap exists at all.

probes.title: No rate limit on chat endpoint — vulnerable to abuse
probes.description: Sends 50 concurrent requests to the chat endpoint with a small prompt. Flags absence of 429 responses combined with most requests succeeding — endpoint can be drained by an attacker with a single IP.

probes.title: Endpoint claims structured output but breaks schema under adversarial input
probes.description: If the endpoint's normal response is JSON-shaped, sends adversarial prompts engineered to break the schema. Flags responses that violate JSON.parse() — schema violations downstream of a 'JSON mode' claim cause real production breakage.

packs.label: MCP Server
packs.tagline: Tool poisoning and capability-escalation probes for MCP servers.
packs.description: Missing transport auth, credential leakage in tool descriptions, and tool-description injection shipped today. Tool-name collision, unbounded resource list, capability escalation via sampling, and more follow.
packs.audience: Teams deploying MCP servers that expose tools to AI hosts.

probes.title: MCP transport accepts unauthenticated tool listings
probes.description: Connects to the MCP server's HTTP/SSE transport without credentials and issues an unauthenticated tools/list JSON-RPC request. Servers that reply with a populated tool catalog leak the full surface to anyone on the internet.

probes.title: Credentials embedded in tool descriptions / parameter schemas
probes.description: Calls tools/list and pattern-matches the returned descriptions and parameter schemas for known secret formats (OpenAI / Anthropic / Stripe / GitHub / generic JWTs). Any AI host that calls these tools sees the credentials in plaintext.

probes.title: Tool description contains instruction-shaped content
probes.description: Calls tools/list and flags descriptions that contain instruction-shaped patterns ("ignore previous", "system:", "</tool>", explicit override keywords). An attacker who controls a tool description can hijack the host model into following arbitrary instructions.

probes.title: Tool name shadows a common built-in / reserved tool
probes.description: Flags tool names that match common built-in names AI hosts use internally (read_file, bash, fetch, etc.). A poisoned MCP server can intercept built-in capability calls in vulnerable hosts.

probes.title: Destructive-named tool exposes no confirmation parameter
probes.description: Identifies tools whose names suggest destructive actions (delete_*, drop_*, send_*) and inspects their inputSchema for a confirmation field. Tools that side-effect without an explicit confirmation parameter rely on the host to gate execution — fragile.

probes.title: Server crashes on malformed tool inputs instead of returning a structured error
probes.description: For each tool, sends malformed payloads (wrong type, oversized string, null arguments). Flags 5xx responses or transport-level errors — well-behaved JSON-RPC servers return error objects, not crashes.

probes.title: Server log notifications leak sensitive tool inputs
probes.description: Invokes a tool with a canary value and inspects the response/log channel for the canary verbatim. Sensitive data echoed in logs leaks into host UIs and observability stacks.

probes.title: resources/list or prompts/list returns unbounded payload
probes.description: Calls resources/list and prompts/list with no pagination cursor and measures response size. Servers that return very large payloads on a single call expose themselves to enumeration-based DoS.

probes.title: Resource URIs accept path-traversal / cross-tenant patterns
probes.description: For each resource URI, attempts path-traversal (../) and cross-tenant query patterns. Flags successful resources/read results — those indicate the server resolves URIs outside the expected scope.

probes.title: Server exposes sampling/createMessage without scoping
probes.description: Calls sampling/createMessage with a basic prompt and no auth context. If the server accepts the call and returns a model response, it can be coerced into making LLM calls on the operator's account — free LLM proxy + cost exposure.

packs.label: Agent Runtime
packs.tagline: Long-horizon adversarial probes for production AI agents.
packs.description: Multi-turn jailbreak escalation, tool-use safety boundaries, memory poisoning, persona drift across sessions.
packs.audience: Production AI agents handling real user sessions.
